intent: Check whether the candidate understands Redis as an in-memory data structure store and can choose a Redis type based on the access pattern instead of treating everything as a string.
primaryQuestion: How would you choose between Redis strings, hashes, lists, sets, sorted sets, and streams for common backend features?

expectedSignals.label: Explains strings for simple values, counters, serialized blobs, and binary-safe data.

requirements.description: Maps Redis strings to simple scalar values, counters, session tokens, flags, or serialized payloads.
Mentions that strings can store binary-safe data or serialized blobs when field-level access is not required.

expectedSignals.label: Uses hashes for small object-like records and field-level updates.

requirements.description: Maps hashes to small object-like records such as user profiles, settings, or per-entity metadata under one key.
Explains that hashes support reading or updating individual fields without replacing the whole serialized object.

expectedSignals.label: Distinguishes lists for ordered push/pop workflows from sets for uniqueness and membership checks.

requirements.description: Uses lists for ordered insertion, push/pop workflows, queues, recent items, or log-like sequences.
Uses sets for uniqueness, membership checks, deduplication, tags, or set operations such as union/intersection.

expectedSignals.label: Uses sorted sets when rank, score, or range queries matter, such as leaderboards or sliding windows.

requirements.description: Uses sorted sets when each unique member needs ordering by score, timestamp, priority, or rank.
Mentions top-N, rank lookup, score update, range query, leaderboard, sliding window, or equivalent operations.

expectedSignals.label: Mentions streams for append-only event logs and consumer-group style processing.

requirements.description: Maps streams to append-only event records where ordered IDs and replayable history are useful.
Mentions consumer groups, multiple workers, acknowledgements, or replay as reasons to choose streams.

expectedSignals.label: Connects type choice to command complexity, memory shape, and required read/write operations.

requirements.description: Chooses the data type from required reads, writes, ordering, uniqueness, score/range queries, or replay needs.
Mentions command complexity, memory layout, object size, or update granularity as a trade-off in the choice.

scoringHints.description: Maps several feature examples to Redis data types, explains why each type fits, and calls out at least one command or complexity trade-off.
Understands the major Redis types and gives plausible examples, but misses performance or operational nuance.
Can name data types but cannot connect them to concrete access patterns.
Answer is generic cache terminology and does not demonstrate Redis-specific modeling knowledge.

followUps.question: For a leaderboard with score updates and top-100 reads, which Redis type would you use and which operations would you expect?
followUps.purpose: Force the candidate to reason about sorted sets and range queries.

followUps.question: When would you avoid storing one large JSON blob in a Redis string and use a hash or JSON document instead?
followUps.purpose: Check awareness of update granularity and memory/performance trade-offs.

en.title: Redis data type selection
en.displayQuestion: How would you choose between Redis strings, hashes, lists, sets, sorted sets, and streams for common backend features?
en.displayIntent: Check practical Redis data modeling from access patterns.
guidance: Ask for concrete feature examples such as sessions, counters, leaderboards, queues, or event logs.
Look for reasoning about operations, ordering, uniqueness, score/range access, and memory shape.
commonMistakes: Treating every Redis value as a serialized string.
Naming data types without explaining the operation they optimize.

vi.title: Chọn kiểu dữ liệu Redis
vi.displayQuestion: Bạn sẽ chọn giữa Redis string, hash, list, set, sorted set và stream như thế nào cho các tính năng backend phổ biến?
vi.displayIntent: Kiểm tra khả năng model dữ liệu Redis theo access pattern thực tế.
guidance: Yêu cầu ví dụ cụ thể như session, counter, leaderboard, queue hoặc event log.
Tìm cách ứng viên lý luận về operation, thứ tự, tính duy nhất, score/range access và memory shape.
commonMistakes: Coi mọi value trong Redis chỉ là một chuỗi serialized.
Kể tên data type nhưng không giải thích operation nào được tối ưu.

ja.title: Redis データ型の選択
ja.displayQuestion: 一般的なバックエンド機能で、Redis の string、hash、list、set、sorted set、stream をどのように選びますか。
ja.displayIntent: アクセスパターンに基づく Redis データモデリングを確認する。
guidance: session、counter、leaderboard、queue、event log など具体例で説明してもらう。
操作、順序、一意性、score/range access、memory shape への言及を見る。
commonMistakes: すべての Redis value を serialized string として扱う。
データ型名だけを挙げ、最適化される操作を説明しない。

intent: Evaluate whether the candidate can explain TTLs, cache-aside flow, stale data, and basic invalidation behavior.
primaryQuestion: How would you implement a Redis cache-aside read path with TTLs, and what can go wrong with stale or missing data?

expectedSignals.label: Describes read-through from Redis first, fallback to the database on miss, then SET with an expiration.

requirements.description: Describes reading Redis first and only querying the database when the cache key is missing or unusable.
Stores the database result back into Redis with an explicit expiration using SET EX/PX or an equivalent TTL operation.

expectedSignals.label: Explains TTL as an upper bound on cache freshness, not a guarantee that data is always correct.

requirements.description: Explains that TTL bounds how long stale cached data may remain after the source data changes.
Avoids claiming TTL alone guarantees correctness for mutable data that can change before expiration.

expectedSignals.label: Mentions explicit invalidation or update-on-write for important mutable data.

requirements.description: Deletes, refreshes, or updates the cache during the write path for data where stale reads matter.
Differentiates frequently changing or correctness-sensitive data from data that can safely rely on TTL alone.

expectedSignals.label: Understands that overwriting a key can affect its timeout depending on the command used.

requirements.description: Mentions that overwriting a key can remove, replace, or preserve expiration depending on the command/options used.
Accounts for TTL behavior when refreshing values so keys do not accidentally become permanent or expire too soon.

expectedSignals.label: Considers thundering herd or cache stampede when many clients miss the same hot key.

requirements.description: Identifies the risk that many concurrent misses for a hot key can overload the database or backend service.
Proposes mitigation such as request coalescing, locking, stale-while-revalidate, TTL jitter, or warming hot keys.

expectedSignals.label: Mentions negative caching carefully for not-found responses with shorter TTLs.

requirements.description: Mentions caching not-found or empty results to reduce repeated database lookups for missing entities.
Uses a short TTL or invalidation strategy for negative results so newly created data is not hidden for too long.

scoringHints.description: Gives a clear cache-aside flow, names stale-data risks, and proposes invalidation plus stampede mitigation.
Explains cache miss and TTL behavior but misses one production concern such as stampede or negative caching.
Knows SET/GET and TTL but treats cache correctness as automatic.
Answer is limited to 'put it in Redis' with no read/write flow.

followUps.question: What TTL would you choose for user profile data versus product price data, and why?
How would you reduce load if a hot key expires and thousands of requests miss at once?
followUps.purpose: Probe stampede mitigation such as jitter, locks, single-flight, or stale-while-revalidate.

en.title: Redis TTL cache-aside flow
en.displayQuestion: How would you implement a Redis cache-aside read path with TTLs, and what can go wrong with stale or missing data?
en.displayIntent: Check practical caching flow, expiration, and stale-data reasoning.
guidance: Ask the candidate to walk through cache hit, cache miss, database read, and cache write.
Push on what happens after the source-of-truth data changes.
commonMistakes: Believing TTL is the same as consistency.
Skipping cache stampede and Redis failure behavior.

vi.title: Cache-aside Redis với TTL
vi.displayQuestion: Bạn sẽ triển khai read path cache-aside bằng Redis với TTL như thế nào, và stale data hoặc missing data có thể gây vấn đề gì?
vi.displayIntent: Kiểm tra luồng caching thực tế, expiration và cách lý luận về stale data.
guidance: Yêu cầu ứng viên đi qua cache hit, cache miss, đọc database và ghi lại cache.
Đào sâu điều gì xảy ra sau khi dữ liệu nguồn thay đổi.
commonMistakes: Nghĩ TTL tương đương với consistency.
Bỏ qua cache stampede và hành vi khi Redis lỗi.

ja.title: TTL 付き Redis cache-aside
ja.displayQuestion: TTL を使った Redis の cache-aside read path をどのように実装し、stale data や missing data では何が問題になりますか。
ja.displayIntent: 実践的な caching flow、expiration、stale data の理解を確認する。
guidance: cache hit、cache miss、database read、cache write を順に説明してもらう。
source of truth のデータが変わった後の扱いを深掘りする。
commonMistakes: TTL を consistency と同じものだと考える。
cache stampede や Redis 障害時の挙動を省略する。

intent: Check whether the candidate can model a simple object in Redis and understand when hashes are better than whole-object string replacement.
primaryQuestion: If you need to cache user profile objects in Redis, when would you store each profile as a hash instead of one serialized string?

expectedSignals.label: Explains hashes as field-value collections under one Redis key.

requirements.description: Explains that a Redis hash stores multiple named fields and values under a single top-level Redis key.
Gives a concrete profile-style example such as user:42 with fields like name, email, avatar, or plan.

expectedSignals.label: Uses hashes when individual fields are read or updated independently.

requirements.description: Chooses a hash when callers often need one or a few fields rather than the whole profile object.
Chooses a hash when individual fields or counters change independently and should not require rewriting the whole blob.

expectedSignals.label: Compares HGET/HSET/HGETALL with GET/SET of a serialized object.

requirements.description: Names hash operations such as HGET, HSET, HMGET, HGETALL, or HINCRBY for field-level access.
Contrasts those operations with GET/SET of one serialized JSON string that must be parsed and rewritten as a whole.

expectedSignals.label: Mentions that hashes are not a replacement for deeply nested relational modeling.

requirements.description: Notes that hashes are flat field maps and are awkward for deep nested arrays or relational relationships.
Keeps complex profile ownership, joins, privacy rules, or source-of-truth constraints in the database or suitable document model.

expectedSignals.label: Discusses key naming, field naming, TTL policy, and source-of-truth ownership.

requirements.description: Defines a predictable Redis key pattern and meaningful field names for the cached profile.
States whether the hash is a cache, its TTL/invalidation policy, and which system owns the authoritative profile data.

expectedSignals.label: Recognizes that very large objects or many fields require memory and command-cost review.

requirements.description: Mentions memory overhead or encoding changes when hashes become very large or contain many fields.
Calls out that broad reads like HGETALL on large hashes can be expensive and should be measured or avoided on hot paths.

scoringHints.description: Explains hash modeling with field-level operations, clear key design, TTL/invalidation, and limits of Redis hashes.
Understands hashes and field updates but misses some operational details.
Uses hashes vaguely as 'objects' without explaining command behavior.
Answer does not show practical Redis key/hash modeling.

followUps.question: What Redis key and hash fields would you create for user id 42?
What changes if the profile has nested arrays, privacy settings, and frequently updated counters?
followUps.purpose: Check when hashes stop being a clean fit.

en.title: Modeling objects with Redis hashes
en.displayQuestion: If you need to cache user profile objects in Redis, when would you store each profile as a hash instead of one serialized string?
en.displayIntent: Check field-level Redis modeling and hash trade-offs.
guidance: Ask for a concrete key and field layout.
Look for comparison between HGET/HSET and GET/SET of serialized JSON.
commonMistakes: Confusing Redis keys with hash fields.
Ignoring update granularity and cache invalidation.

vi.title: Model object bằng Redis hash
vi.displayQuestion: Nếu cần cache user profile object trong Redis, khi nào bạn lưu mỗi profile dưới dạng hash thay vì một serialized string?
vi.displayIntent: Kiểm tra khả năng model Redis ở mức field và trade-off của hash.
guidance: Yêu cầu một layout key và field cụ thể.
Tìm phần so sánh giữa HGET/HSET và GET/SET serialized JSON.
commonMistakes: Nhầm Redis key với hash field.
Bỏ qua update granularity và cache invalidation.

ja.title: Redis hash で object を表現する
ja.displayQuestion: Redis に user profile object を cache する場合、1 つの serialized string ではなく hash として保存するのはどんな時ですか。
ja.displayIntent: field-level の Redis modeling と hash の trade-off を確認する。
guidance: 具体的な key と field の layout を説明してもらう。
HGET/HSET と serialized JSON の GET/SET の比較を見る。
commonMistakes: Redis key と hash field を混同する。
更新粒度と cache invalidation を無視する。

sourceReferences.note: Used for hash field-value modeling.

intent: Assess whether the candidate can reason about ordered versus unique collections in Redis using lists, sets, and sorted sets.
primaryQuestion: How would you decide whether a feature should use a Redis list, set, or sorted set?

expectedSignals.label: Uses lists for insertion-order sequences, queue-like push/pop, or recent activity windows.

requirements.description: Uses lists when insertion order or recency order is the main requirement.
Mentions push/pop or trim-style operations such as LPUSH/RPUSH/LPOP/RPOP/LTRIM for queues or recent activity.

expectedSignals.label: Uses sets for unique membership, deduplication, and set algebra such as union or intersection.

requirements.description: Uses sets when uniqueness or deduplication is required and element order is not central.
Mentions membership checks or set operations such as SISMEMBER, SADD, SUNION, or SINTER.

expectedSignals.label: Uses sorted sets when unique members also need ordering by score or rank.

requirements.description: Uses sorted sets when members must stay unique but also be ordered by score, timestamp, priority, or rank.
Mentions range, rank, top-N, score update, or reverse-order reads as sorted set operations.

expectedSignals.label: Identifies that list membership checks are not the same as set membership checks.

requirements.description: Explains that a list preserves sequence but does not provide set-style unique membership semantics.
Chooses set or sorted set when efficient membership/dedup lookup matters instead of scanning a list.

expectedSignals.label: Mentions trimming bounded collections such as recent events or leaderboards.

requirements.description: Defines a maximum size or retention window for recent lists, leaderboards, sliding windows, or similar collections.
Mentions trimming, removing old scores, TTLs, or retention jobs so Redis collections do not grow without bounds.

expectedSignals.label: Gives at least two concrete examples such as notification inbox, tags, leaderboard, or sliding rate limit.

requirements.description: Provides at least two concrete backend feature examples instead of only naming Redis data structures.
For each example, explains the ordering, uniqueness, rank, or membership requirement that drives the chosen type.

scoringHints.description: Clearly separates ordering, uniqueness, and scoring requirements and maps each to the correct Redis structure.
Chooses reasonable structures but misses one edge case such as trimming or membership cost.
Knows the names but confuses list, set, and sorted-set semantics.
Answer is too abstract to confirm Redis collection knowledge.

followUps.question: For a 'recently viewed products' feature, how would you avoid duplicates while keeping recency order?
followUps.purpose: Probe combined requirements and possible sorted set usage.

followUps.question: How many items can this collection grow to, and how would you cap it?
followUps.purpose: Check operational awareness of unbounded data structures.

en.title: Lists, sets, and sorted sets
en.displayQuestion: How would you decide whether a feature should use a Redis list, set, or sorted set?
en.displayIntent: Check collection semantics: order, uniqueness, and score-based ranking.
guidance: Ask for examples where a wrong type would create bugs.
Look for bounded-growth thinking, not only command names.
commonMistakes: Using lists for uniqueness without a deduplication plan.
Using sets when the feature requires rank or recency order.

vi.title: List, set và sorted set

vi.displayIntent: Kiểm tra ngữ nghĩa collection: thứ tự, tính duy nhất và ranking theo score.
guidance: Hỏi ví dụ mà chọn sai type sẽ gây bug.
Tìm tư duy giới hạn growth, không chỉ tên command.
commonMistakes: Dùng list cho uniqueness nhưng không có kế hoạch deduplicate.
Dùng set khi feature cần rank hoặc recency order.

ja.title: list、set、sorted set
ja.displayQuestion: ある機能で Redis list、set、sorted set のどれを使うべきか、どのように判断しますか。
ja.displayIntent: collection の意味、order、uniqueness、score-based ranking を確認する。
guidance: 誤った型選択が bug になる例を聞く。
command 名だけでなく、bounded growth への意識を見る。
commonMistakes: 重複排除の計画なしに list で uniqueness を扱う。
rank や recency order が必要なのに set を使う。

intent: Assess understanding of Redis persistence options and when persistence matters for cache versus durable data use cases.
primaryQuestion: What are the practical differences between Redis RDB snapshots, AOF, using both, and disabling persistence?

expectedSignals.label: Explains RDB as point-in-time snapshots with lower write overhead but possible data loss between snapshots.

requirements.description: Explains that RDB persists periodic point-in-time snapshots rather than every individual write.
Mentions the risk of losing writes made after the latest successful snapshot.

expectedSignals.label: Explains AOF as logging write operations with stronger durability options and rewrite/size considerations.

requirements.description: Explains that AOF records write commands and replays them during recovery.
Mentions fsync policy, file growth, rewrite cost, disk I/O, or restart-time trade-offs for AOF.

expectedSignals.label: Recognizes that cache-only deployments may disable persistence if the data can be rebuilt.

requirements.description: Recognizes that persistence can be disabled when Redis stores only rebuildable cache or derived data.
Considers rebuild time, database load, cold-start behavior, and user impact before disabling persistence.

expectedSignals.label: Mentions combining RDB and AOF for different recovery and durability goals.

requirements.description: Mentions that RDB and AOF can be combined to balance snapshot recovery speed with stronger write durability.
Connects the combined setup to recovery objectives such as startup time, data-loss window, or operational safety.

expectedSignals.label: Connects persistence choice to recovery time, acceptable data loss, disk I/O, and operational complexity.

requirements.description: Defines acceptable data-loss window, recovery time, or business impact before selecting a persistence mode.
Accounts for disk throughput, fsync latency, rewrite behavior, memory overhead, or operational monitoring.

expectedSignals.label: Avoids claiming Redis persistence makes it equivalent to a relational durable database for all workloads.

requirements.description: Avoids claiming Redis persistence provides the same guarantees as relational transactions and constraints.
Identifies workflows that still need a durable source of truth, idempotency, or database constraints outside Redis.

scoringHints.description: Compares RDB, AOF, both, and no persistence using durability, recovery, disk I/O, and use-case fit.
Knows RDB and AOF basics but gives limited operational trade-offs.
Can define persistence but cannot pick a strategy for a real use case.
Answer contains incorrect claims about Redis durability.

followUps.question: For session storage, what data-loss window is acceptable and how would that affect RDB or AOF settings?
What would you monitor after enabling AOF on a write-heavy Redis instance?
followUps.purpose: Check disk, latency, rewrite, and recovery awareness.

en.title: Redis persistence trade-offs
en.displayQuestion: What are the practical differences between Redis RDB snapshots, AOF, using both, and disabling persistence?
en.displayIntent: Check whether the candidate can choose Redis persistence based on data-loss tolerance and recovery needs.
guidance: Ask for separate answers for cache-only data and user-session data.
Listen for disk I/O, recovery time, and acceptable data loss.
commonMistakes: Assuming Redis persistence has no trade-off.
Using durable settings for rebuildable cache data without a reason.

vi.title: Trade-off persistence trong Redis
vi.displayQuestion: Khác biệt thực tế giữa Redis RDB snapshot, AOF, dùng cả hai và tắt persistence là gì?
vi.displayIntent: Kiểm tra khả năng chọn persistence dựa trên mức chấp nhận mất dữ liệu và nhu cầu recovery.
guidance: Yêu cầu trả lời riêng cho cache-only data và user-session data.
Lắng nghe các ý về disk I/O, recovery time và acceptable data loss.
commonMistakes: Nghĩ Redis persistence không có trade-off.
Dùng durable setting cho dữ liệu cache có thể rebuild mà không có lý do.

ja.title: Redis persistence の trade-off
ja.displayQuestion: Redis の RDB snapshot、AOF、両方の利用、persistence 無効化にはどのような実務上の違いがありますか。
ja.displayIntent: data loss tolerance と recovery needs に基づいて persistence を選べるか確認する。
guidance: cache-only data と user-session data で別々に答えてもらう。
disk I/O、recovery time、acceptable data loss への言及を見る。
commonMistakes: Redis persistence に trade-off がないと考える。
再構築可能な cache data に理由なく durable setting を使う。

intent: Assess practical debugging of stale cache, wrong keys, TTL mistakes, and cache-aside invalidation issues.
primaryQuestion: Users report seeing old product data even after the database was updated. How would you debug the Redis cache path?

expectedSignals.label: Checks whether the request is reading from Redis, database, or another cache layer.

requirements.description: Verifies whether the affected request path reads from Redis, the database, CDN, in-process cache, or another cache layer.
Compares the expected product read flow with logs, traces, or configuration to avoid debugging the wrong layer.

expectedSignals.label: Inspects the exact cache key, value, TTL, and serialization/version shape.

requirements.description: Looks up the exact Redis key used by the product request and inspects its value, TTL, and existence state.
Checks serialization format, schema version, product id, tenant, locale, or cache version embedded in the cached value.

expectedSignals.label: Verifies write path invalidation, cache update ordering, and whether deletes are reaching Redis.

requirements.description: Verifies that product update code deletes or refreshes the matching Redis key and that the command succeeds.
Checks whether the database commit, cache delete, cache refill, and event processing order can produce stale values.

expectedSignals.label: Considers race conditions where an old read repopulates the cache after an update.

requirements.description: Considers a race where an in-flight stale read repopulates Redis after the product update or invalidation.
Uses timestamps, logs, traces, cache write events, or value versions to prove whether stale repopulation occurred.

expectedSignals.label: Checks environment or tenant key prefixes to avoid looking at the wrong Redis keyspace.

requirements.description: Checks environment, region, namespace, or deployment prefix so debug commands target the same Redis keyspace as production traffic.
Checks tenant, store, locale, or product variant components in the key to avoid inspecting the wrong cached object.

expectedSignals.label: Proposes a safe remediation such as targeted invalidation, shorter TTL, versioned keys, or write-through for critical fields.

requirements.description: Proposes a focused fix such as invalidating affected keys, correcting the key pattern, shortening TTL, or versioning cache entries.
Escalates to write-through, stronger invalidation, or bypassing cache for correctness-sensitive product fields when needed.

scoringHints.description: Gives a structured debug plan from symptom to key inspection, invalidation path, race analysis, and low-risk remediation.
Checks key/value/TTL and invalidation, but misses race or multi-layer cache details.
Suggests clearing cache but lacks root-cause investigation.
Answer is not actionable for a real stale-cache incident.

followUps.question: How would you prove whether the stale value was repopulated after the database update?
followUps.purpose: Probe timeline, logging, and race-condition analysis.

guidance: Tìm phần kiểm tra cụ thể key, TTL, value và write-path invalidation.
Đào sâu cách khắc phục an toàn thay vì xóa cache diện rộng.
commonMistakes: Flush Redis production ngay từ bước đầu.
Bỏ qua race repopulate cache sau database write.

intent: Evaluate whether the candidate can select and reason about Redis maxmemory and eviction policies under memory pressure.
primaryQuestion: How would you choose a Redis maxmemory-policy for an application cache, and what metrics would tell you the policy is wrong?

expectedSignals.label: Explains maxmemory as a configured memory ceiling for the dataset and eviction trigger.

requirements.description: Explains that maxmemory sets a Redis memory ceiling after which Redis applies the configured eviction behavior.

expectedSignals.label: Compares noeviction, allkeys-lru, allkeys-lfu, volatile-lru, volatile-ttl, and random policies at a practical level.

intent: Assess understanding of Redis command atomicity, transactions, WATCH-based optimistic locking, and Lua scripts.
primaryQuestion: How would you make a multi-step Redis update atomic, and when would you use MULTI/EXEC, WATCH, or Lua?

expectedSignals.label: Explains MULTI/EXEC queues commands and executes them sequentially as one isolated operation.

expectedSignals.label: Explains WATCH as optimistic locking that aborts EXEC if watched keys change before execution.

expectedSignals.label: Mentions that long-running scripts block other server activity and should stay small.

requirements.description: Mentions that Redis runs Lua scripts atomically and long scripts can block other commands.

expectedSignals.label: Understands that Redis transactions do not provide rollback semantics like many SQL databases.

scoringHints.description: Clearly separates single-command atomicity, transaction isolation, optimistic locking, and Lua server-side branching with caveats.

intent: Evaluate whether the candidate understands Redis-based locking guarantees, expiration, ownership tokens, and failure modes.

intent: Assess practical debugging of Redis memory growth, latency spikes, and hot-key or large-key problems.
primaryQuestion: Redis latency suddenly increased and memory is near the maxmemory limit. How would you investigate before changing configuration?

expectedSignals.label: Starts with symptoms and scope: affected commands, endpoints, nodes, time window, and recent deploys or traffic changes.

requirements.description: Identifies affected endpoints, commands, Redis nodes, tenants, time window, and severity before changing configuration.
Checks recent deploys, traffic shifts, feature flags, data imports, or infrastructure changes that correlate with the latency increase.

expectedSignals.label: Checks INFO memory/stats, hit/miss ratio, evicted_keys, expired_keys, connected clients, command stats, and slow logs.

requirements.description: Checks Redis INFO memory/stats, commandstats, SLOWLOG, connected clients, and latency-related diagnostics.
Checks hit/miss ratio, evicted_keys, expired_keys, used_memory, memory fragmentation, and app error or latency metrics.

expectedSignals.label: Looks for large keys, hot keys, unbounded collections, missing TTLs, or accidental key prefix explosions.

requirements.description: Looks for large keys, unbounded lists/sets/streams, sudden key cardinality growth, or prefix explosions.
Looks for hot keys, missing TTLs, TTL distribution problems, or access skew that explains latency or memory pressure.

expectedSignals.label: Considers eviction pressure, persistence/AOF rewrite, replication buffers, fork overhead, and network/client backpressure.

requirements.description: Considers eviction churn, AOF rewrite, RDB snapshots, fork copy-on-write overhead, or disk I/O as latency sources.
Considers replication backlog, client output buffers, slow consumers, network saturation, or connection pressure.

expectedSignals.label: Proposes targeted mitigation such as adding TTLs, trimming collections, fixing key cardinality, batching carefully, or scaling memory.

requirements.description: Chooses mitigations based on observed evidence rather than immediately changing maxmemory or eviction policy.
Mentions specific fixes such as adding TTLs, trimming collections, splitting hot keys, limiting cardinality, careful batching, or scaling capacity.

expectedSignals.label: Avoids unsafe commands on production without sampling or using safer tooling.

requirements.description: Avoids production-wide blocking commands such as KEYS or huge HGETALL/SMEMBERS scans on hot paths.
Uses safer tooling such as SCAN, redis-cli --bigkeys, sampling, replicas, or controlled diagnostics.

scoringHints.description: Provides a production-safe investigation plan covering metrics, keyspace shape, command behavior, persistence/replication, and targeted mitigation.
Covers core Redis metrics and key inspection but misses one operational factor.
Suggests obvious fixes without enough diagnosis.
Answer would increase production risk during an incident.

intent: Evaluate senior-level understanding of Redis Cluster key distribution, hash slots, multi-key limitations, and operational trade-offs.
primaryQuestion: How does Redis Cluster distribute keys, and how would that affect key design for multi-key operations?

expectedSignals.label: Explains that Redis Cluster splits the keyspace into 16384 hash slots and assigns slots to primary nodes.

requirements.description: States that Redis Cluster divides keys across 16384 hash slots.
Explains that slot ranges are assigned to primary nodes and determine where keys live.

expectedSignals.label: Mentions CRC16-based slot mapping and hash tags for forcing related keys into the same slot when needed.

requirements.description: Mentions that Redis derives a key's slot from hashing the key, commonly described as CRC16 modulo 16384.
Uses hash tags such as cart:{userId}:items to intentionally colocate related keys in the same slot.

expectedSignals.label: Understands clients should follow MOVED/ASK redirections and maintain slot-to-node mapping.

requirements.description: Mentions MOVED or ASK redirections during cluster routing or resharding.
Explains that clients should be cluster-aware and maintain or refresh slot-to-node mappings.

expectedSignals.label: Discusses limitations and complexity around multi-key commands, transactions, or Lua scripts across different slots.

requirements.description: Identifies that many multi-key commands, transactions, or Lua scripts require keys to be in the same slot.
Designs key names with hash tags or alternative data modeling when atomic multi-key operations are required.

expectedSignals.label: Connects key design to data co-location, hot slots, uneven load, resharding, and read/write scaling.

requirements.description: Balances colocating related keys against the risk of concentrating too much hot traffic into one hash slot.
Connects key naming to slot balance, resharding cost, read/write scaling, and uneven node load.

expectedSignals.label: Mentions failover with replicas and operational concerns during resharding or node replacement.

requirements.description: Mentions replica promotion or failover behavior for Redis Cluster primaries.
Mentions operational risks during resharding, node replacement, slot migration, or client redirection churn.

scoringHints.description: Explains slots, hash tags, client routing, cross-slot constraints, hot-slot risk, and operational resharding/failover implications.
Understands hash slots and basic sharding but misses hot-key or client-routing nuance.
Knows Cluster scales Redis but cannot design keys for multi-key operations.
Answer does not demonstrate Redis Cluster mechanics.

followUps.question: If you need to update cart:{userId}:items and cart:{userId}:total atomically, how would you name the keys in Cluster?

intent: Assess senior-level judgment about using Redis for durable or correctness-critical workflows, including persistence, replication, and application-level safeguards.
primaryQuestion: For a correctness-critical workflow, how would you decide whether Redis is acceptable as the primary store or only as a cache/coordination layer?

expectedSignals.label: Starts from business correctness requirements: acceptable data loss, duplicate processing, stale reads, and recovery objectives.

requirements.description: Defines acceptable data loss, duplicate side effects, stale reads, and recovery objectives before choosing Redis as primary storage.
Connects those tolerances to user-visible harm, money movement, compliance, inventory, entitlement, or other business consequences.

expectedSignals.label: Compares Redis persistence and replication guarantees with database constraints, transactions, and consensus-backed systems.

requirements.description: Compares Redis persistence/replication with relational constraints, transactions, isolation, and durable commits.
Mentions consensus-backed systems, queues, or databases when the workflow needs stronger coordination than Redis provides.

expectedSignals.label: Mentions asynchronous replication and failover windows that can lose acknowledged writes.

requirements.description: States that Redis replication is commonly asynchronous and can lag behind acknowledged primary writes.
Explains that a failover can promote a replica that has not received the latest acknowledged writes.

expectedSignals.label: Uses Redis safely as cache, idempotency aid, rate limiter, or derived state when source of truth exists elsewhere.

requirements.description: Uses Redis safely when an authoritative database, log, or external system can reconstruct or validate the state.
Identifies Redis roles such as cache, idempotency helper, rate limiter, coordination aid, or derived/ephemeral state.

expectedSignals.label: Proposes durable source-of-truth patterns such as relational constraints, append-only logs, outbox, or transactional writes when needed.

requirements.description: Proposes relational constraints, transactional writes, append-only logs, outbox, or event store patterns for critical state.
Includes idempotency, reconciliation, replay, or compensation so Redis failures do not create unrecoverable bad outcomes.

expectedSignals.label: Defines verification through tests, failure drills, monitoring, and documented SLO/SLA assumptions.

requirements.description: Defines tests or failure drills for Redis restart, failover, data loss, duplicate processing, and stale reads.
Documents monitoring, alerts, SLO/SLA assumptions, recovery runbooks, and accepted risk for the chosen design.

scoringHints.description: Frames the decision around correctness requirements and maps Redis strengths and limits to a defensible architecture.
Recognizes Redis limits and suggests a source of truth, but lacks failure-drill or observability depth.
Mentions persistence but does not reason about correctness-critical failure modes.
Answer overclaims Redis guarantees.

followUps.question: What is the maximum acceptable data loss and duplicate side effect for this workflow?
If Redis failover loses a recently acknowledged write, what application-level design prevents a bad user-visible outcome?

intent: Assess senior incident diagnosis for hot keys, hot slots, cluster imbalance, and cache stampede under high traffic.
primaryQuestion: A Redis Cluster has one overloaded primary while other nodes are healthy. How would you diagnose and mitigate the issue?

expectedSignals.label: Checks whether load is due to a hot key, hot hash slot, uneven slot assignment, client routing problem, or command pattern.

requirements.description: Distinguishes hot key, hot hash slot, uneven slot distribution, client routing issue, or expensive command pattern as possible causes.
Checks cluster topology, slot ownership, node roles, and whether the overloaded primary owns disproportionate hot slots.

expectedSignals.label: Correlates node CPU, commandstats, network, latency, key access distribution, and application endpoints.

requirements.description: Correlates CPU, network, commandstats, slowlog, latency, memory, and per-node Redis metrics.
Correlates Redis load with application endpoints, tenants, campaigns, deploys, or traffic patterns.

expectedSignals.label: Inspects key naming and hash tags for accidental co-location of high-traffic keys.

requirements.description: Inspects Redis key names for hash tags that intentionally or accidentally force many keys into the same slot.
Identifies whether high-traffic keys share a constant tag, tenant tag, or other key pattern that creates a hot slot.

expectedSignals.label: Considers large-key operations, unbounded collections, cache stampede, and write amplification.

requirements.description: Considers large values, big hashes/sets/lists/streams, unbounded collection growth, or expensive full-key reads.
Considers cache stampede, request fan-out, repeated writes, or write amplification as causes of one-node overload.

expectedSignals.label: Proposes mitigations such as key splitting, local caching, request coalescing, TTL jitter, sharding high-cardinality keys, resharding slots, or moving hot paths.

requirements.description: Proposes immediate mitigations such as local caching, request coalescing, TTL jitter, rate limiting, or bypassing a hot path.
Proposes structural mitigations such as key splitting, high-cardinality sharding, resharding slots, or redesigning key tags.

expectedSignals.label: Balances immediate mitigation with long-term data-model and workload changes.

requirements.description: Separates urgent user-impact reduction from longer-term data model, workload, or cluster topology changes.
Mentions operational risk, rollout plan, compatibility, or validation needed when changing key design or resharding.

scoringHints.description: Separates hot key, hot slot, uneven sharding, and command-pattern causes, then proposes staged mitigations with blast-radius control.
Identifies hot-key or slot issues but misses client/app correlation or long-term prevention.
Suggests scaling Redis without diagnosing load concentration.
Answer lacks Cluster-specific incident reasoning.

followUps.question: What immediate mitigation could reduce user impact while you redesign the cache key model?

intent: Evaluate senior-level design of Redis-backed rate limiting with algorithm choice, atomicity, memory bounds, and abuse cases.
primaryQuestion: How would you design a Redis-backed rate limiter, and how would you choose between fixed window, sliding window, and token bucket approaches?

expectedSignals.label: Compares fixed window simplicity with boundary burst problems.

requirements.description: Explains fixed window limiting as a simple counter per time bucket with TTL.
Explains how traffic near two adjacent window boundaries can exceed the intended steady limit.

expectedSignals.label: Explains sliding window using sorted sets or counters with finer time buckets and the memory/cost trade-off.

requirements.description: Describes sliding window implementation using timestamps in sorted sets or finer-grained bucket counters.
Mentions memory, cleanup, command cost, or cardinality trade-offs compared with fixed windows.

expectedSignals.label: Explains token bucket or leaky bucket semantics for smoothing bursts and replenishment.

requirements.description: Explains token bucket behavior with a refill rate and burst capacity.
Connects token/leaky bucket semantics to smoothing bursts or enforcing sustained request rates.

expectedSignals.label: Ensures atomic check-and-update with Lua or carefully chosen Redis primitives.

requirements.description: Ensures checking the current allowance and recording the new request happen atomically.
Uses Lua, MULTI/EXEC with care, INCR+EXPIRE patterns, or sorted-set operations that avoid race conditions.

expectedSignals.label: Defines keys by actor, route, tenant, IP, or user and sets TTLs to bound memory.

requirements.description: Defines limiter keys by actor dimensions such as user, IP, tenant, route, API key, or login target.
Uses TTLs or cleanup so per-actor rate limiter state does not grow without bounds.

expectedSignals.label: Discusses fail-open versus fail-closed behavior, observability, abuse patterns, and distributed deployment consistency.

requirements.description: Chooses fail-open or fail-closed behavior based on endpoint risk, such as login abuse versus public catalog reads.
Covers monitoring, abuse detection, alerting, multi-instance consistency, and what happens when Redis is degraded.

scoringHints.description: Compares algorithms by user experience, correctness, atomicity, memory bounds, and operational failure behavior.
Designs a workable limiter but misses one senior concern such as fail-open policy or memory bound.
Knows Redis counters but cannot handle concurrency or boundary bursts.
Answer is not safe under real concurrent traffic.

expectedSignals.label: Identifies root cause with evidence such as memory, eviction, latency, command stats, replication lag, hot keys, or application deploys.

requirements.description: Uses Redis evidence such as memory, eviction, latency, commandstats, slowlog, replication lag, or hot-key data to support the root cause.
Correlates Redis evidence with application logs, deploy timing, traffic, database load, or incident timeline.

expectedSignals.label: Explains actions taken, trade-offs considered, rollback plan, and communication during the event.

requirements.description: Explains the concrete remediation actions and why they were chosen over alternatives.
Mentions rollback plan, stakeholder/customer communication, incident coordination, or operational handoff.

expectedSignals.label: Quantifies impact using metrics such as p95/p99 latency, hit rate, error rate, database load, cost, recovery time, or incident duration.

requirements.description: Provides before/after or during/after metrics such as p95/p99 latency, hit rate, error rate, DB load, or throughput.
Provides a comparison time window, affected volume, incident duration, recovery time, cost change, or traffic scale.

expectedSignals.label: Mentions follow-up prevention such as dashboards, alerts, runbooks, key design changes, TTL policy, load tests, or failure drills.

requirements.description: Mentions dashboards, alerts, SLOs, or runbooks added to detect similar Redis issues earlier.
Mentions preventive design or validation changes such as key model fixes, TTL policy, load tests, capacity planning, or failure drills.

expectedSignals.label: Can explain what they personally owned versus what teammates or platform teams handled.

requirements.description: Clearly states which diagnosis, code/config change, rollout, communication, or follow-up work the candidate personally owned.
Separates personal contributions from teammates, SRE/platform teams, managed service support, or leadership decisions.

scoringHints.description: Gives a specific incident or migration with evidence, actions, trade-offs, measured outcome, and prevention work.
Has credible production experience but lacks some measurement or ownership clarity.
Story is plausible but too generic or missing proof of impact.
Cannot substantiate the Redis experience claimed on the CV.

followUps.question: What exact metric moved after your change, and over what time window did you compare it?
What was your rollback plan if the Redis change made latency or correctness worse?

en.title: Redis production ownership
en.displayQuestion: Tell me about a Redis production incident, migration, or scaling change you owned. What happened, what did you change, and how did you prove it worked?
en.displayIntent: Verify hands-on Redis production experience with evidence and measurable impact.
guidance: Ask for topology, metrics, timeline, and the candidate's personal role.
Look for prevention work after the immediate fix.
commonMistakes: Giving a generic caching story with no metrics.
Skipping rollback, communication, and prevention details.

vi.title: Ownership Redis trong production
vi.displayQuestion: Hãy kể về một incident, migration hoặc scaling change Redis trong production mà bạn đã own. Chuyện gì xảy ra, bạn thay đổi gì, và bạn chứng minh nó hiệu quả như thế nào?
vi.displayIntent: Xác thực kinh nghiệm Redis production bằng evidence và impact đo được.
guidance: Hỏi topology, metric, timeline và vai trò cá nhân của ứng viên.
Tìm prevention work sau khi fix trước mắt.
commonMistakes: Kể chuyện cache chung chung không có metric.
Bỏ qua rollback, communication và prevention detail.

ja.title: production Redis ownership
ja.displayQuestion: あなたが owner として対応した Redis production incident、migration、または scaling change について話してください。何が起き、何を変更し、どう効果を証明しましたか。
ja.displayIntent: evidence と measurable impact によって Redis production experience を確認する。
guidance: topology、metrics、timeline、candidate の個人 role を聞く。
immediate fix 後の prevention work を見る。
commonMistakes: metrics のない一般的な caching story に終始する。
rollback、communication、prevention details を省略する。
